text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 03 October 2018

Dr Amelia Shah
Holloway Road Medical Practice

145 Holloway Road, London, N7 8LX

Dear Dr Amelia Shah

Wendy Brown, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: K482715 NHS No. 6592841037
Flat 7, Brookside Avenue, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 2QD

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset. Longstanding history with prior EEGs suggesting focal onset; previous MRI (2006) reportedly without an epileptogenic lesion.

Reason for review: Pre‑conception counselling.

History since last contact:
I reviewed Wendy Brown today for pre‑conception counselling. She attended alone and reports good general health. She and her partner are planning pregnancy in the next 6–12 months. She keeps a detailed seizure diary and, in the past four weeks, she has recorded four absence seizures and one myoclonic this month. She notes these tend to cluster around periods of poor sleep and higher stress at work. There have been no reported focal to bilateral tonic–clonic seizures, no injuries, and no hospital attendances.

Current treatment:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily (well tolerated; no dose changes in the last year)
- Folic acid 5 mg once daily (started three months ago)
- Vitamin D 1000 IU daily (self‑initiated)

Previous anti‑seizure medications (historical): Levetiracetam (mood changes), carbamazepine (dizziness), valproate (stopped years ago; not currently used), lacosamide (fatigue), gabapentin (ineffective). No known exposure to topiramate, zonisamide, perampanel, pregabalin, or clobazam in recent years.

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Pre‑conception counselling discussion:
We discussed general optimisation prior to pregnancy, including seizure safety, sleep regularity, adherence, and avoidance of alcohol. We reviewed the importance of continuing high‑dose folic acid until at least 12 weeks’ gestation and ideally pre‑conception, which she is already taking. We also discussed the need to avoid valproate in pregnancy unless no suitable alternative exists; she is not on valproate. We covered driving regulations, risks of seizures in pregnancy, and the need for early antenatal referral to an obstetric team familiar with epilepsy.

Examination: Well, alert, oriented, normal speech. No focal neurological deficits. BMI within healthy range. Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg.

Investigations/records reviewed: Prior MRI brain (2006) reported normal. Historical video‑EEG telemetry consistent with focal onset. No recent lab abnormalities on GP screening (as per records forwarded).

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; maintain strict adherence. We discussed that any future dose change in pregnancy should be guided by clinical need and lamotrigine levels where available.
- Continue folic acid 5 mg daily pre‑conception and through the first trimester.
- Seizure diary to continue; she will report any increase in frequency or change in pattern. She understands to contact us if the four absence seizures and one myoclonic this month pattern worsens or if she experiences any focal to bilateral tonic–clonic events.
- Arrange lamotrigine level prior to conception and again in early pregnancy if/when confirmed.
- Pre‑pregnancy bloods via GP (U&Es, LFTs, Vitamin D if not recently checked) and routine antenatal referral once pregnant.
- Provide written epilepsy‑in‑pregnancy advice and safety leaflet.
- Follow‑up: nurse specialist telephone review in 8 weeks; clinic review in 4–6 months or sooner if pregnant.

Thank you for your continued care. Please let me know if there are any concerns or if medication adjustments are considered in primary care so we can coordinate.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: in the past four weeks, she has recorded four absence seizures and one myoclonic this month
four absence seizures and one myoclonic this month

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: "in the past four weeks, she has recorded four absence seizures and one myoclonic this month." This indicates a total of 5 seizures in the past four weeks (i.e., one month). There are no other conflicting frequencies. Therefore, the normalized frequency is 5 per month.
seizure_frequency_number: in the past four weeks, she has recorded four absence seizures and one myoclonic this month
reference: four absence seizures and one myoclonic this month
